rationale: The page provides factual information about the Booker Prize, its history, eligibility criteria, and specific Indian-origin winners, which is relevant for cultural and literary history topics.
cleanedText:
The Booker Prizes
Award Details:
- Awarded for: Best work of sustained fiction of the year, written in English and published in the UK or Ireland
- Location: Somerset House, Strand, London, England
- Country: United Kingdom
- Presented by: Booker, McConnell Ltd (1969–2001); Man Group (2002–2019); Crankstart (2019 onwards)
- Reward(s): £50,000
- First awarded: 1969
Indian Winners of the Booker Prize:
- 1997: Arundhati Roy - The God of Small Things
- 2006: Kiran Desai - The Inheritance of Loss
- 2008: Aravind Adiga - The White Tiger
About Booker Prize:
- Founded in 1969, the Booker Prize is open to English-language novels from any country that have been published in Britain and Ireland.
- It aimed to stimulate the reading and discussion of contemporary fiction.
- Some Indian-origin authors who won the prize include VS Naipaul (1971), Salman Rushdie (1981), Arundhati Roy (1997), among others.
Note: Booker Prize is given for fiction books written in English only whereas International Booker Prize is awarded for books translated into English.
